danger_signals.meaning: Immediate physical danger to player character

safety_signals.visual_form: Friendly character models in story sequences
safety_signals.meaning: Support network; mission objective (rescue Yuri indicates safe outcome when achieved)

safety_signals.visual_form: Intermission stages (Beer Bottle Cut/generic bottle destruction, Super Fire Blow training)
safety_signals.meaning: Resource gain; narrative respite; power progression opportunity

safety_signals.meaning: Organized competition framework; shifting from criminal threat to sport; decreased danger narrative

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Tournament fighters manipulated by Geese's forces

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Sakazaki family legacy; martial arts mastery
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Training sequences; quest locations; rescue mission narratives

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Yuri missing from playable roster; Mr. Big stage presence; narrative urgency in character dialogue/cutscenes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ryo and Robert move through hostile stages defeating Mr. Big's forces; progression toward rescue confrontation

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mafia control and manipulation (Art of Fighting 2)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tournament setting; Geese Howard as omnipresent threat; urban criminal aesthetic across stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ryo and allies engage in tournament battles while uncovering Geese's manipulation; shift from rescue mission to conspiracy revelation

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Transition from criminal to sporting (Art of Fighting 2)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: King of Fighters tournament bracket replaces criminal hideouts; formalized competition venues
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Characters compete honorably in tournament format rather than fighting criminals; narrative tone shifts from danger to rivalry

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Glasshill Valley location; desert/rural aesthetic; Tom Wyler's elixir research
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Robert searches for Freia Lawrence; new cast of characters; different geographic threat from prior urban settings

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mr. Karate unmasking scene; Takuma Sakazaki reveal as enigmatic final boss
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Narrative cliffhanger; family secret disclosure; recontextualization of threat as familial conflict

environmental_storytelling.example: Mac's Bar (Japan) renamed to Mac's Cafe (US/EU) communicates regional content policies and world-state interpretation of alcohol in narrative spaces

King character's clothing damage (Japanese: significant shirt destruction revealing brassiere; US/EU: unbuttoned but intact shirt) communicates regional danger/safety norms and character vulnerability states
Urban criminal locations (bars, mafia hideouts) visually establish Mr. Big and Geese Howard's territorial control and threat presence
Beer Bottle Cut (Japan) vs generic bottle destruction (US/EU) and Super Death Blow vs Super Fire Blow renaming communicates power progression and regional content interpretation
Yuri's absence as playable character until Art of Fighting 3 communicates her kidnapping state and narrative role transition
Mr. Big's cigarette removal and 'Jeff Bogard disposed/defeated' language change (vs 'killed') conveys tonal shifts in narrative danger and resolution

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Commands criminal forces; serves as stage boss; criminal organization leader visual presentation
Masked appearance creating narrative mystery; confrontation triggers identity revelation; father-son conflict narrative

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Protagonist on rescue mission (AoF1) / Tournament competitor (AoF2)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Progresses through stages defeating enemies; accepts tournament challenges; martial arts mastery displays through victory animations
Supports Ryo in story progression; becomes playable character; shares Kyokugenryu Karate techniques
Orchestrates tournament; reveals mafia connection; drives fighter manipulation narrative; represents organized crime threat
Fights normally; reveals gender through damage state when defeated by certain moves; represents power concealment narrative
Compete in King of Fighters tournament; unaware of Geese Howard's control; behavior changes when conspiracy revealed

notes: Art of Fighting communicates world-state primarily through narrative framing and character progression rather than dynamic environmental shifts. The SNES version shows significant regional censorship affecting visual danger signals (alcohol references, character damage, violence language). The series progression from Art of Fighting 1→2→3 shows shift from criminal kidnapping crisis → mafia conspiracy/tournament → new geographic threat. Character roster visibility (Yuri's absence/presence) serves as narrative state indicator. The Mr. Karate identity reveal represents culminating danger-to-family-conflict recontextualization. Bonus game framing (spirit gauge training) provides mechanical world-state progression markers. Faction identity relies heavily on character models and stage locations rather than color coding or emblems.
